coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for two-photon photodynamic cancer therapy with a low fs laser power density
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jing
Zhang, Zehui
Zha, Shuai
Zhu, Yinyan
Wu, Peiyi
Ehrenberg, Benjamin
Chen, Ji-Yao
coredata.dc:description: The 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl 4-pyridinio) porphyrins (TMPyP), a photosensitizer used for photodynamic therapy of cancers (PDT), were linked to carbon dots (CDots) to form the conjugates of CDot–TMPyP by the electrostatic force. The 415 nm emission band of CDots was well overlapped with the absorption band of TMPyP, so that the Cdots in conjugates can work as donor to transfer the energy to TMPyP moiety by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with an FRET efficiency of 45%, determined by the fluorescence lifetime change between the free CDots and conjugated CDots. The two-photon absorption cross section (TPACS) of TMPyP is as low as 110 GM and the TMPyP thus be not suitable for two-photon PDT. Whereas the CDots have high TPACS, and their TPACS are excitation wavelength dependent with the maximum value of 15000 GM at 700 nm. Therefore, the conjugates of CDot–TMPyP were explored for two-photon excitation (TPE) PDT. The two-photon image of CDot–TMPyP in Hela cells was clearly seen under the excitation of a 700 nm femto-second (fs) laser. The singlet oxygen production of CDot–TMPyP was also much higher than that of TMPyP alone under TPE of a 700 nm fs laser. The in vitro PDT killing was further achieved with CDot–TMPyP by TPE of the 700 nm fs laser. Particularly herein the low power density of fs laser from unfocused laser beam was successfully used to carry out the TPE PDT, because of the high TPACS of CDots. These results demonstrate that the CDot–TMPyP conjugates are promising for TPE PDT and needed to investigate further.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Two-photon absorption cross section
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Photodynamic therapy of cancers

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TPE spectral measurement of CDots and TMPyP
TPACS measurements of CDots and TMPyP
Cell culture and cell treatments with CDot-TMPyP
Imaging measurements of CDots–TMPyP in cells by one-photon excitation (OPE)
Imaging measurements of CDot-TMPyP in cells by TPE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDots. The inset in A) presents the diameters' distribution of CDots. (B) FTIR spectrum of CDots. (C) AFM topography image of the CDots on a mica substrate; (D) the corresponding height-profile analysis along the line in C).
(A) The molecular structure of TMPyP; (B) The absorption and emission spectra of TMPyP (2.8 μg/mL) and CDots (5 μg/mL).

(A) Zeta potential measurements of free CDots and the CDot–TMPyP conjugates. (B) Fluorescence lifetime curves of free CDots and the conjugated CDots (10 μg/mL) with different amount of TMPyP in aqueous solutions, excited by a 405 nm ps laser and measured by TCSPC with a 405–450 nm band-pass filter.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of free CDots (2.5 μg/mL), CDot–TMPyP (2.5 μg/mL−1.75 μg/mL) and TMPyP (1.75 μg/mL) in aqueous solutions. Excitation: 360 nm. (B) Fluorescence excitation spectra of CDots (2.5 μg/mL), CDot–TMPyP (2.5 μg/mL−1.75 μg/mL) and TMPyP (1.75 μg/mL) in aqueous solutions. The emission wavelengths were fixed at 415 nm and 670 nm, for CDots and TMPyP, respectively.

Fluorescent images of each compound in Hela cells under OPE of a 405 nm CW laser. (A) Cells were incubated with TMPyP (7 μg/mL) for 2 h. (B) Cells were incubated with CDots (10 μg/mL) for 2 h. (C) Cells were incubated with CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) for 2 h. Column 1 shows the CDot imaging channel, detected at the wavelength region of 410–450 nm. Column 2 shows the TMPyP imaging channel, detected with the 590 nm long pass region. Column 3 shows the DIC images. Column 4 shows the merged images. The bar size is 20 μm.

(A) TPE induced fluorescence emission spectra of Rhodamine B (20 μm), CDots (1.16 μm) and TMPyP (100 μm) at different excitation wavelengths with the same 400 mW power of the fs laser. (B) Logarithmic plots of the dependence of relative TPE induced fluorescence of CDots on the irradiation power of a fs laser at 700 nm. The fitted slope is 2.07. The estimated uncertainty of the fitted slope is ±0.1. (C). The picture of the Cdots fluorescence in the laser beam's path.

TPE fluorescence images in HeLa cells excited by the 700 nm fs laser. (A) The cells were incubated with CDots (10 μg/mL) for 2 h. (B) Cells were incubated with TMPyP (7 μg/mL) for 2 h. (C) Cells were incubated with CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) for 2 h. Upper row with the mark 1: detection channel for CDot fluorescence acquired with the filter of 370–450 nm; Bottom row with the mark 2: detection channel for TMPyP acquired at wavelengths of 610–675 nm. The bar size is 20 μm.

Decrease of the fluorescence intensity of DPBF (18 μm) with the irradiation time under irradiation with 700 nm fs laser, in the presence of the studied compounds. (A) TMPyP (7 μg/mL); (B) CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL); (C) CDot–TMPyP(10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) and 1 mm NaN3; (D) A comparison of DPBF photo-degradations under the irradiation of 700 nm fs laser.

The TPE PDT damaging on Hela cells, measured by an MTT assay. Except for the control groups, the cells were incubated with TMPyP (7 μg/mL), CDots (10 μg/mL) or CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) for 2 h, respectively, followed by the irradiation of the 700 nm fs laser with a power density of 160 mW/mm2 for different times.
Schematic illustration of the CDot–TMPyP preparation and FRET mediated photosensitization by TPE with a 700 nm fs laser.
TPACS of CDot and TMPyP at selected wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots featuring efficient FRET for two-photon photodynamic cancer therapy with a low fs laser power density

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Surface Physics and Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of Micro and Nano Photonic Structures (Ministry of Education), Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Surface Physics and Department of Physics, and Key Laboratory of Micro and Nano Photonic Structures (Ministry of Education)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Department of Macromolecular Science, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Institure of Nanotechnology, Bar Ilan University, Ramat Gan 52900, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Institure of Nanotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl 4-pyridinio) porphyrins (TMPyP), a photosensitizer used for photodynamic therapy of cancers (PDT), were linked to carbon dots (CDots) to form the conjugates of CDot–TMPyP by the electrostatic force. The 415 nm emission band of CDots was well overlapped with the absorption band of TMPyP, so that the Cdots in conjugates can work as donor to transfer the energy to TMPyP moiety by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with an FRET efficiency of 45%, determined by the fluorescence lifetime change between the free CDots and conjugated CDots. The two-photon absorption cross section (TPACS) of TMPyP is as low as 110 GM and the TMPyP thus be not suitable for two-photon PDT. Whereas the CDots have high TPACS, and their TPACS are excitation wavelength dependent with the maximum value of 15000 GM at 700 nm. Therefore, the conjugates of CDot–TMPyP were explored for two-photon excitation (TPE) PDT. The two-photon image of CDot–TMPyP in Hela cells was clearly seen under the excitation of a 700 nm femto-second (fs) laser. The singlet oxygen production of CDot–TMPyP was also much higher than that of TMPyP alone under TPE of a 700 nm fs laser. The in vitro PDT killing was further achieved with CDot–TMPyP by TPE of the 700 nm fs laser. Particularly herein the low power density of fs laser from unfocused laser beam was successfully used to carry out the TPE PDT, because of the high TPACS of CDots. These results demonstrate that the CDot–TMPyP conjugates are promising for TPE PDT and needed to investigate further.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a local light-activated treatment to destroy tumor tissues [1,2]. The principle of PDT is that the photosensitizer (PS) accumulates in malignant tissues, and the subsequent activation of the PS by light with appropriate wavelength generates singlet oxygen (1O2), which destroys the lesion [3]. An efficient PDT photosensitizer requires achieving the following conditions: (1) efficient uptake by the cancer cells or tissue, (2) a high quantum yield for singlet oxygen generation, (3) strong light absorption in the red region which is close to the tissue optical window (700–900 nm), (4) non-toxicity in the absence of light exposure and (5) photostability [4]. The 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl 4-pyridinio) porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP) is known to be an efficient water-soluble PS, because it is a positively charged molecule and is taken up easily by cells which usually have negative surface potential [5–7]. Moreover, the 1O2 yield of TMPyP is high (ф Δ = 0.77) making TMPyP very attractive in PDT studies [8]. However, TMPyP is still a porphyrin derivative with the main absorption band around 420 nm and relatively weak absorption bands in the visible and the red regions. This drawback greatly limits the PDT applications of TMPyP, because the short wavelength light cannot penetrate deeply into living tissues under common one-photon excitations (OPE) [9]. Alternatively, two-photon excitation (TPE) using near-infrared (NIR) wavelength light has been suggested as TPE PDT for PDT improvement, since the light in NIR region has the best penetration depth into living tissues [10]. In TPE PDT, the PS simultaneously absorbs two less-energetic photons, that elevate it into the excited electronic state [11]. This proposes that we could use NIR wavelengths to excite the PS, which has absorption bands in the UV/visible range. Since the concept of TPE PDT was proposed in 1990s [12], several in vitro studies have been tested and positive PDT results were obtained with the focused fs laser beam in a microscope setup [13,14]. This results from the fact that the TPE threshold for laser power density must be satisfied to allow the PS to absorb two-photons simultaneously. This strict condition generally limits the occurrence of TPE. The TPE threshold depends on the two factors. One is the TPACS of the PS, and the other is the irradiation photon density (power density). If the TPACS of the PS is low, the irradiation photon density must be high to fulfill the requirement of TPE. However the TPACS of commonly used PS are all lower than 100 GM [15,16]. This is the reason why TPE PDT in previous reports was achieved only with heavily focused laser beams. Concerning the in vivo PDT applications, using a focused laser beam is not a suitable modality for practical PDT applications, because focusing laser beams deep inside tissues is unrealistic, due to strong scattering of tissues. The TPACS of TMPyP was reported to be low [17], so that the TMPyP alone has no potential for TPE PDT.

With the rapid development of nanotechnology in the last two decades, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have become a new type of fluorescence probes with a high TPACS. However, although QDs fit well for TPE, the heavy metal elements contained in QDs, such as Cd, have serious toxicity effects to a living body so that the applications of these QDs raised health and environmental concerns [18–20]. Fortunately, with the help of surface modification carbon nanodots (CDots) were found as good fluorescence agents in 2006 [21]. These CDots soon attracted much attention for biomedical studies [22,23], because CDots have higher biocompatibility and less toxicity compared to semiconductor QDs [24–28]. Due to the high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, promising luminescence properties, non-blinking fluorescent emission [29,30], and especially their large TPACS [31], CDots have become a rising star in biomedical applications [32].

In this work, we explored a new application of CDots for PDT. We prepared conjugates of CDot–TMPyP and utilized FRET characteristic from Cdots to TMPyP in conjugates to produce 1O2 under irradiation of NIR fs lasers. The TPACS of CDots is two orders of magnitude higher than that of TMPyP, so that the CDots in such conjugates can be effectively excited by TPE of NIR fs laser and then transfer the energy to TMPyP moiety, producing 1O2. The Cdot-TMPyP further carried out the FRET mediated PDT in Hela cancer cells under TPE of 700 nm fs laser and destroyed cells effectively. Particularly, the unfocused fs laser beam with a low power density of 160 mW/mm2 was successfully used in this in vitro experiment to achieve a remarkable cell damaging effect. The TPACS of CDots reaches a high level of 15000 GM and therefore the power density of fs laser can be reduced accordingly. These results highlight the potential of CDot–TMPyP for FRET-mediated TPE PDT with NIR wavelength.

The 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methyl 4-pyridinio) porphyrin tetra(p-toluenesulfonate) (TMPyP) was obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO) and used without further purification. The compound was dissolved in water or phosphate buffer solution at pH 7.4. The size of the CDots was determined by a TEM instrument at 200 kV (JEOL JEM-2010F). The morphologies of the CDots were measured by AFM (Solver P47-MDT) in contact mode in air. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDots as well as the TMPyP were measured spectrophotometrically (Hitachi U-500 and Hitachi F-2500). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDots were obtained using an IRPRESTIGE-21spectroscope (Shimadzu) with KBr pellets. The zeta potentials of the CDots and CDot–TMPyP were measured in water solutions using a Malvern Zetasizer (ZEN 3600, Worcestershire, UK).

The CDots were prepared by a well-established hydrothermal treatment according to a previous report [33]. Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (2 g, 9.5 mmol) and l-cysteine (1 g, 8.3 mmol) were dissolved in water (5 mL), followed by drying at 70 °C overnight. Then the pale yellow syrup was redispersed in water (25 mL) and heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 6 h.

The TMPyP molecule has four positive charges in its four benzene rings (Fig. 2A ), so that it can bind on the surfaces of the negatively charged CDots by an electrostatic interaction. To prepare the CDot–TMPyP conjugates, different amounts of TMPyP were added into the CDots suspension (10 μg/mL) and the mixture was stirred overnight in the dark at room temperature (25 °C). We then measured FERT efficiencies of these conjugates that were made at different molar ratios by measuring the fluorescence lifetime changes of CDot–TMPyP relative to that of free CDots to find the optimal ratio.

Time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) is based on the detection of single photons of a periodical light signal and the reconstruction of the waveform from the time measurements. A 2 × 107 Hz 405 nm pico-second (ps) laser (Edinburgh Instruments, EPL405) was used to excite the CDots or CDot–TMPyP in aqueous solution. The fluorescence decay courses of the CDots were measured by a PMT (Hamamatsu, R928P) with a band-pass filter of 405–450 nm in the TCSPC (Edinburgh Instruments, TCC900). The obtained decay curves can be fitted with a multi-exponential decay as described in Equation (1):
(1)
I
( t )
=
∑
i = 1

t
τ i
)
Where
τ i
and
α i
represent the decay constant and amplitude of each exponential component. With repeated measurements, the average PL lifetimes of the CDots in different cases were determined, by using Equation (2) [34]:
(2)
τ ¯
=
∑
i = 1
n
α i
τ i 2
∑
i = 1
n
α i
τ i

When
τ 0
and τ , the average lifetimes of the CDots alone and in conjugates were obtained, respectively, the FRET efficiency of the CDot–TMPyP conjugate was calculated by Equation (3) [35]:
(3)
E = 1 −
τ
τ 0

ce:section.ce:section-title: TPE spectral measurement of CDots and TMPyP
ce:section.ce:para: A high-performance mode-locked Ti: sapphire laser (Spectra Physics Mai Tai eHP) of a 100 fs pulse width and 80 MHz repetition rate was used for two-photon excitation (TPE). The wavelength of the output laser beam could be tuned from 700 to 1040 nm. We focused the laser beam with a 350 mm focal length lens onto a cuvette containing the sample solution. The focal spot was about 200 μm in diameter. The induced fluorescence was directed onto the entrance slit of a spectrometer (Acton, Spectropro 2150i). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a liquid nitrogen-cooled CCD (Princeton, Spec-10:100B LN) that was mounted on the spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TPACS measurements of CDots and TMPyP
ce:section.ce:para:
The TPACS of CDots and TMPyP were measured by comparing the TPE induced fluorescence of CDots or TMPyP against that of the reference Rhodamine B, based on the Equation (4) [36]:
(4)
F =
K Ф c l δ
Ι 2
/ 2
where δ is the TPACS.
δ r
of the reference Rhodamine was taken to be 210 GM (1 GM = 10−50 cm4 s/photon) [37], δ of the CDots can be expressed in terms of
δ r
and other parameters in Equation (5)
(5)
δ =
F
F r
Ф r
C r
Ф C
δ r

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cell treatments with CDot-TMPyP
ce:section.ce:para: Human epithelial cervical cancer cells (HeLa) were obtained from the cell bank of Shanghai Science Academy. The cells were seeded in culture dishes containing DMEM medium with 10% calf serum, 100 units mL−1 penicillin, 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin and 100 μg mL−1 neomycin, and incubated in a fully humidified incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2. When the cells reached 80% confluence with normal morphology, the compound was added and the cell dish was placed in the incubator for the desired time. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS three times to remove unassociated compounds, and then these washed cell samples were ready for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging measurements of CDots–TMPyP in cells by one-photon excitation (OPE)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence images of free CDots, free TMPyP and CDot–TMPyP conjugates in Hela cells were measured in a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) (FV300, IX71, Olympus Microscopy, Tokyo, Japan) excited by a 405 nm continuous wave laser. In these measurements, the CDots' fluorescence was measured in detection channel 1 of the LSCM with a band-pass filter of 410–450 nm, and the fluorescence signal of TMPyP was recorded in detection channel 2 with a 590 nm long pass filter. Differential interference contrast (DIC) images were obtained simultaneously in a transmission channel to exhibit the cells' morphology. A water immersion objective ( × 60) and a matched pinhole of LSCM were used in experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging measurements of CDot-TMPyP in cells by TPE
ce:section.ce:para: The TPE fluorescence images of free CDots, free TMPyP, and CDot–TMPyP in cells were collected through a water immersion objective (20×, 1.00 N.A.) of a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM, FV1000, Olympus, Inc.) with a band-pass filter of 360–450 in detection channel 1 for CDots and 610–675 nm in the detection channel 2 for TMPyP. The excitation NIR fs laser was coupled into the LSCM. The excitation laser power on the cells was about 5 mW (62.5 pJ/pulse), as measured by a power meter (Newport, Model No. 1918-C).

Singlet oxygen (1O2) plays a main role in PDT. We use 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) probe, a sensitive probe of 1O2, to detect the 1O2 produced by the CDot–TMPyP via FRET under the TPE of the 700 nm fs laser. The 700 nm fs laser beam from a Ti: Sapphire laser (Coherent, Mira 900 B), with a repetition rate of 80 MHz and an average power of 500 mW, was used for the irradiation in the experiments. The DPBF can be quickly oxidized by 1O2 to become o-dibenzoylbenzene, leading to the degradation of the DPBF with a reduced fluorescence intensity. The loss of the DPBF can be measured by the reduction of the DPBF fluorescence intensity at the 490 nm band, when excited by 405 nm in a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-2500). In the experiment, the DPBF (18 μm) was mixed with TMPyP (7 μg/mL), CDots (10 μg/mL), or CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) in an aqueous solution, and then irradiated by a 700 nm fs laser for different times, such as 1 min, 2 min etc. The fluorescence intensity decrement of the DPBF is usually used as the indicator of the 1O2 production during the photosensitization process of PS. The degradation rate of the DPBF with irradiation time is proportional to the 1O2 yield, and thus can be used to evaluate the 1O2 yields for different compounds.

The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) tetrazolium reduction assay was used to measure the PDT effects of the CDot–TMPyP on cells. The Hela cells were cultured in culture dishes (35 mm in diameter). When the cells reached 80% confluence with a normal morphology, one of the compounds such as CDots, TMPyP or CDot–TMPyP was added to the culture dish and incubated for a desired time. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS three times to remove the unassociated compound, and fresh medium was added. These cells were then digested by pancreatin for 3 min and the cell suspension was collected into a sterile glass tube. The cell suspension in the tube was irradiated by the 700 nm fs laser. After irradiation, the cells were equally seeded in some wells of a 96 well at bottom tissue culture plate with 104 cells for each well. This 96 well culture plate was incubated for 24 h, and then 10 mL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well for incubation of 1.5 h. Finally, the 96 well plates were put into an iEMS Analyzer (Lab-system) to measure the optical densities (O.D.) at 450 nm, for each well. The cell viability in each well was determined by comparing the O.D. value with that of untreated control cells in other wells in the same plate. All the results are presented as the mean ± SE from three independent experiments with four wells in each.

Due to the low TPACS of PS, the high power density of a focused fs laser beam on the target was usually used in previous TPE studies to carry out TPE PDT. However, focusing the laser deep within the tumor tissue is unrealistic due to the strong scattering by the tissue, so that this focusing manner seems unfit for practical TPE PDT applications. In this work, we intended to use the CDot–TMPyP conjugates, to achieve TPE PDT with a low power density from an unfocused fs laser beam, because the CDots have very high TPACS relative to that of PS. This strategy may have potential for practical TPE PDT. The design of this work is shown in Scheme 1 .

A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1A) shows that the prepared CDots are relatively uniform in size with an average diameter of 8 nm. The construction of the CDots was further characterized by atom force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 1C, D). The images suggest that the CDots have disk-like structure with the size of about 8 nm in diameter and 2.5 nm in height. Moreover, the surface functional groups of CDots are characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). FTIR spectrum shows the presence of NH2, SH, COOH and OH groups. Bands at 1570, 1507, and 1453 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC stretching mode of the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The peak at 3050–3400 cm−1 is usually assigned as the = C–H/N–H/O–H stretching modes. The sharp band at 1709 cm−1 is attributed to the CO/COOH stretching mode, whereas the broad band centered at 1200–1310 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–C/C–N/C–O, suggesting that the surfaces of CDots are covered with –COOH and –OH groups [38,39].

Due to the negative charges on the carboxyl terminal of the CDots in neutral solution environment (pH = 7.4), the TMPyP is easily bound on the surfaces of the CDots to form the CDot–TMPyP conjugates via electrostatic interaction [40], since the TMPyP is a tetra-cationic porphyrin (Fig. 2A). In homogenous aqueous solutions at pH 7.4 the TMPyP is characterized by an absorption maximum of the Soret band at 420 nm and a fluorescence band around 670 nm (Fig. 2B). The emission band of CDots (donor) locates at 415 nm, which is well-overlapped with the absorption band of the TMPyP (acceptor), creates a satisfactory condition for FRET from CDots to TMPyP in conjugates. Taking Rhodamine B as the reference sample, the fluorescence quantum yields of CDots and TMPyP were measured as 0.67 and 0.15, respectively.

For a selected CDots concentration (10 μg/mL), various ratios of TMPyP: CDots were tested to find the optimal conjugates and finally a mass ratio of 10 μg/mL (CDots) to 7.0 μg/mL (TMPyP) was selected for conjugation, because at this ratio the TMPyP molecules were almost totally conjugated on the CDots surfaces measured by the fluorescence of left TMPyP in supernatants after centrifugation. The conjugation was confirmed by the zeta potential measurements of CDots and CDot–TMPyP. Fig. 3A shows that the Zeta potential of unconjugated CDots is −17 mV, demonstrating that the free CDots have negative charges on their surfaces. In contrast, the zeta potential of the CDot–TMPyP conjugates was to +5 mV, reflecting that the TMPyP molecules have covered the CDots surfaces (Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B exhibits the fluorescence lifetime changes between the free CDots and several CDot–TMPyP samples that were prepared with different mass ratios of the two components. The fluorescence lifetime change of the donor in the conjugates relative to the donor in a free state is taken as a standard parameter to evaluate the FRET efficiency. With the increased mass ratio of CDots to TMPyP, the fluorescence lifetime of conjugated Cdots in CDot–TMPyP decreased accordingly, and the shortest fluorescence lifetime was found to be 8.3 ± 0.24 ns at the mass ratio of 10 μg/mL (CDots) to 7.0 μg/mL (TMPyP). Comparing to the average fluorescence lifetime T 0 = 14.8 ± 0.61 ns of free CDots, the FRET efficiency (E) of the CDot–TMPyP reaches 45% according to Equation (3) E = 1−T/T 0. The mass ration of 10 μg/mL (CDots) to 7.0 μg/mL (TMPyP) is equal to a molar ratio of 0.058 μM:5 μm, indicating that about 100 TMPyP molecules were attached on each CDot averagely.

The FRET effect in conjugates can be checked by fluorescence intensity changes of the donor and the acceptor in the conjugates relative to that in a free state, respectively. The fluorescence spectra and intensities of the free CDots, TMPyP and CDot–TMPyP conjugates, with the same concentrations in aqueous solutions, were measured under excitation at 360 nm (Fig. 4A). The fluorescence emission of the free CDots is strong, since the fluorescence quantum yield of the CDots reaches 0.7 and the CDots have a strong absorption at 360 nm (Fig. 2B). The emission intensity of the free TMPyP is weak, because the absorption of TMPyP at 360 nm is low (Fig. 2B) and the fluorescence quantum yield of the TMPyP is less than 2%. The emission intensity (415 nm) of the conjugated CDots in CDot–TMPyP obviously reduced while the TMPyP emission intensity (670 nm) of the CDot–TMPyP increased, as compared with that of the free CDots and free TMPyP, demonstrating the typical FRET model. The fluorescence excitation spectra and intensities of these compounds are shown in Fig. 4B when their emissions were fixed as 415 nm and 670 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence changes were in agreement with that in Fig. 4A, confirming the FRET function of CDot–TMPyP.

The cellular uptakes of these compounds were studied with Hela cancer cells by fluorescence imaging technique. The fluorescence images of free Cdots, TMPyP and CDot–TMPyP in cells are shown in Fig. 5 under excitation of a 405 nm CW laser beam. After 2 h incubation with TMPyP (7 μg/mL), the TMPyP fluorescence was found in the nucleus region of cells (Fig. 5A), which is consistent with previous studies [41,42]. In contrast, the cellular CDtos existed only in the cytoplasm of cells after incubation of CDots (10 μg/mL) for 2 h (Fig. 5B). As nanoparticles with a size of 8 nm, the CDots are too big and therefore cannot pass through the nuclear pores to enter the nucleus. The fluorescence image of cellular CDot–TMPyP exhibited that the CDot–TMPyP complex distributes in the cytoplasm with a granule-like pattern, which may be indicative of accumulation in lysosomes (Fig. 5C). Similar to that of CDots alone, the big size of CDot–TMPyP prevents their penetration into the nucleus. As shown in Fig. 5C, The excellent fluorescence imaging overlapping of the CDots and TMPyP for cellular CDot–TMPyP conjugates conforms that the CDots and TMPyP still remained in the conjugate form in the cells after the CDot–TMPyP have penetrated into cells. This suggests that the stable CDot–TMPyP conjugates in the cellular environment can further perform FRET in cells.

Based on above results the basic optical properties of CDot–TMPyP have been established. The CDot–TMPyP conjugates were then further studied for TPE. The first factor for TPE is the TPACS of the compound. Several groups have reported that CDots have very high TPACS, but they measured only TPACS of CDots at 800 and 810 nm [31,43]. From the absorption spectrum of CDots (Fig. 2B), we can see that CDots have an absorption peak at 350 nm and therefore the maximum TPACS of CDots was expected to be around 700 nm. Herein we measured TPACS of CDots in aqueous solution by TPE induced fluorescence at the wavelengths of fs laser from 700 nm to 800 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the TPE induced fluorescence of CDots sensitively depends on the excitation wavelength. Taking Rhodamine B as a reference sample, the TPACS of CDots at different excitation wavelengths as well as the TPACs of TMPyP are listed in Table 1 . We also checked the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDots and excitation power of fs laser. As shown in Fig. 6B, the square dependence of fluorescence intensity against the excitation power confirms the TPE process. The important finding here is that the TPACS of CDots at 700 nm is 10 times higher than that at 800 nm. This is the first report to our knowledge, and this would certainly enhance the TPE application of CDots. Fig. 6C demonstrates that the bright blue fluorescence (in web version) of the CDots can be clearly seen even with naked eyes in the light path of the 700 nm fs laser.

Although the TPACS of TMPyP is small, the TPACS of CDots is two orders magnitude higher so that the CDot–TMPyP conjugates possess the possibility of effective two-photon excitation of NIR fs laser via FRET. Fig. 7 depicts TPE fluorescence images of these compounds in Hela cells. These cells have been incubated with free CDots (10 μg/mL), TMPyP (7 μg/mL) or CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL- 7 μg/mL) for 2 h Fig. 7A1 shows the bright fluorescence image of cells that were incubated with CDots, since the CDots can easily enter the cells and the TPACS of these CDots reaches 15 000 GM. In contrast, the fluorescence image of cells that were incubated with TMPyP is very dim (Fig. 7B2), because the TPACS of the TMPyP is low and its fluorescence quantum yield is low as well. However, for cells that were incubated with CDot–TMPyP, the intense fluorescence image of the conjugated TMPyP can be seen (Fig. 7C2) whereas the relatively weaker fluorescence image of conjugated CDots (Fig. 7C1) (in contrast to Fig. 7A1) was found, demonstrating that the cellular CDot–TMPyP can be effectively excited by TPE of the NIR fs laser. This result also reflects that the FRET occurred in the cellular CDot–TMPyP conjugates under 700 nm TPE of the fs laser, resulting in an enhanced fluorescence of the conjugated TMPyP and a decreased fluorescence intensity of the conjugated CDots. The effective excitation of the conjugated TMPyP in cellular CDot–TMPyP via the FRET under the 700 nm TPE would facilitate photodynamic therapy.

In this in vitro work, the cell incubation times for TMPyP and CDot–TMPyP were identical (2 h), because we found that the CDot–TMPyP and free TMPyP have similar cellular penetrating rates, resulting in a similar concentration of compounds in the cells following incubation. This finding was taken from the result of the following fluorescence measurement. The TMPyP was added to the cell dish (106 cells) with an incubation concentration of 7 μg/mL. After 2 h incubation time, a part of the TMPyP was endocytosed by cells while the other remained in the solution medium. The solution medium in the cell dish was then extracted, and its fluorescence intensity was measured at the excitation of 420 nm in a spectrometer and compared with that of the original TMPyP with a concentration of 7 μg/mL. By measuring the remaining fluorescence of the TMPyP left in the solution medium, we can estimate the TMPyP amount in the cells. The measurement for the CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) incubated cells was carried out in parallel. Finally, we found that after 2 h cell incubation time of CDot–TMPyP or free TMPyP the amounts that were in the solution medium of the cell dishes are almost the same, reflecting that the TMPyP amounts in TMPyP incubated cells is similar with that of CDot–TMPyP incubated cells. In this fluorescence comparison measurement, we wanted to compare the left TMPyP amount in solution medium and left CDot–TMPyP amount in solution medium after their incubation to cells, respectively. Therefore in this fluorescence measurement, the excitation wavelength was selected as 420 nm. The 420 nm wavelength cannot induce FRET in the CDot–TMPyP and can only directly excite the conjugated TMPyP, creating a fair condition for the comparison of the free TMPyP and CDot–TMPyP.

Under TPE of NIR fs laser, the conjugated TMPyP in CDot–TMPyP was excited via the FRET route. The TMPyP in the excited state can either emit fluorescence, returning back to the ground state, or intersystem-cross producing 1O2 via energy transfer to the ground state O2 by collision. Since the 1O2 production quantum yield of TMPyP is high (0.77), once the TMPyP was excited the 1O2 production would be remarkable. The ability of CDot–TMPyP under TPE of NIR fs laser to produce 1O2 was checked by the 1O2 probe DPBF. The two-photon effects, including two-photon induced fluorescence and the production of 1O2 are proportional to the square of the irradiation power density. However, the high irradiation power density can directly damage the tissues and cells, so that an unfocused laser beam with a moderate power density is preferred for practical PDT treatments. Here, the 700 nm fs laser with an average power of 500 mW was used. The sample, in a glass tube, was irradiated by this unfocused laser beam with an irradiation spot in 2 mm diameter, so that the irradiation power density was 160 mW/mm2. Due to the high TPACS of CDots, this low power density can still meet the threshold of TPE. During the irradiation the sample tube was shaken to allow the compounds in solution to be fully irradiated.

The oxidation of DPBF by 1O2 leads to a decrease in the DPBF fluorescence, and the decreasing rate of DPBF fluorescence is usually used to evaluate the relative 1O2 yield. In the experiment, the CDots (10 μg/mL), TMPyP (7 μg/mL) or CDot–TMPyP conjugate (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL) were mixed with DPBF (18 μm) and then the mixtures were irradiated by the 700 nm fs laser. Fig. 8 B shows the decreasing curves of the DPBF fluorescence with the irradiation time (min) of 700 nm fs laser for CDot–TMPyP, and Fig. 8A gives that for TMPyP alone. The comparison of DPBF degradation by the free TMPyP, CDots and CDot–TMPyP are summarized in Fig. 8D. There was almost no DPBF degradation for the case of CDots, because CDots have little ability to produce 1O2. The DPBF degradation for TMPyP group was slight, because of the low TPACS of TMPyP. However, the reduction of the DPBF fluorescence for CDot–TMPyP group was so evident indicating that CDot–TMPyP photo-produced a large amount of 1O2 under the irradiation of the 700 nm fs laser. Sodium azide (NaN3), a physical quencher of 1O2, was used in experiments to further confirm the 1O2 production. As shown in Fig. 8C, with a NaN3 concentration of 1 mm in the CDot–TMPyP solution, the decrease of DPBF fluorescence was significantly suppressed under the equal irradiation of 700 nm fs laser, demonstrating that the NaN3 quenched 1O2, preventing the oxidation of DPBF. The results of Fig. 8B and C clearly demonstrate that the CDot–TMPyP can effectively produce 1O2 under TPE of NIR fs laser, particularly with a low power density.

The FRET-mediated cell inactivation by PDT of the 700 nm TPE was subsequently studied in HeLa cells. The incubation concentration of each compound and the incubation time were the same (2 h) as those in the above imaging measurements. The 500 mW unfocused 700 nm fs laser beam (2 mm in diameter) was used to irradiate the cell suspension in the glass tube with an irradiation power density of 160 mW/mm2. The cells in each cell dish were incubated by either the CDots (10 μg/mL) or TMPyP (7 μg/mL) or CDot–TMPyP (10 μg/mL-7 μg/mL), digested and collected in different glass tubes. During the irradiation, the glass tube was shaken to allow homogeneous irradiation. After irradiation, the cells were equally seeded in each well of a 96 well at bottom tissue culture plate and incubated for 24 h, and then an MTT solution was added to each well, to measure the cells' viability. The unirradiated control cells in the cell suspensions were also put into some of this 96 well culture plate with an equal cell density of irradiated cells and treated in the same way, for comparison. Fig. 9 showed the PDT damaging effects on Hela cells.

Under the irradiation of the 700 nm fs laser, no significant damaging could be found for the CDots-incubated cells. This result can be understood as the CDots are not producers of 1O2. The damaging effect of TMPyP alone is slight. Although many TMPyP molecules have entered the cells (Fig. 5A), the low TPACS of TMPyP made the TPE PDT ineffective. The TPE PDT effect of CDot–TMPyP is obvious, as most cells have been killed after 45 min irradiation of the 700 nm fs laser. In addition, the damaging rate of CDot–TMPyP was found to be proportional to the irradiation time of the 700 nm fs laser, showing a typical light-dose dependent PDT mode. With this low power density of 160 mW/mm2 from unfocused fs laser beam, effective damaging of cancer cells by CDot–TMPyP was achieved, demonstrating that the CDot–TMPyP conjugates have a great potential for TPE PDT.

The TMPyP was a well-established PS. Differing from other commonly used PSs such as sulfonated aluminum phthalocyanine (AlPcS) and chlorin e6 (Ce6), the TMPyP is a positively charged PS and therefore the cellular uptake of TMPyP is much better than that of AlPcS and Ce6. In addition, the 1O2 quantum yield of TMPyP is also higher than that of AlPcS and Ce6, making TMPyP much efficient relative to AlPcS and Ce6 in the in vitro PDT studies. On the other hand, both AlPcS and Ce6 have an absorption Q band at the red light region so that they frequently used in the in vivo PDT studies and the AlPcS has been approved for practical PDT in clinic. Whereas the TMPyP only has an absorption band at the violet region and thus the TMPyP does not fit the red light PDT in practice. However when TMPyP is conjugated with Cdots to form Cdot-TMPyP, the Cdot-TMPyP can not only overcome the weakness of poor light absorption of TMPyP at red region by TPE of NIR fs laser but also facilitate the 1O2 production because of the high 1O2 quantum yield of TMPyP and the extremely high TPACS of Cdots. Therefore, the Cdot–TMPyP conjugates with TPE of NIR fs laser are promising for practical PDT applications and needed to investigate further.

Although TPE PDT with NIR lasers was believed to be a good way for PDT developments, this modality requires the sensitizing compounds to have a large TPACS. Since commonly used PSs all have the small TPACS, these PSs are not efficient for TPE PDT. Designing and developing new PSs with the high TPACS is a route to promote TPE PDT. However establishing a new PS is a systematical work. Not only the TPACS is concerned but other properties of new PS, including the 1O2 yield, stability, biocompatibility and toxicity should be thoroughly studied and satisfied for practical PDT treatments, making the establishment of new PS for this purpose a difficult long-term task. In this work, we designed another strategy to improve the TPE PDT. TMPyP, a well-established PS was selected to connect electrostatically with non-toxic CDots to form conjugates. Based on the good spectral matching between CDots and TMPyP, the FRET efficiency of CDot–TMPyP reached 45%. The FRET mediated PDT of CDot–TMPyP under TPE of NIR fs laser can be successfully carried out to achieve destruction of the cancer cells. Our results suggest that the conjugates of high TPACS nanoparticles with an established PS could be a simple and feasible modality to improve TPE PDT for practical PDT applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (11074053 and 31170802) is gratefully acknowledged.
